content:
AI now does in-house what companies used to buy outside. The old make-or-buy question has a simple new answer: make.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every company lives with one old question: do we make this ourselves, or do we pay someone outside to do it? For my whole career in project management and software and hardware development, the answer came out of a calculus so stable that nobody questioned it.
AI now answers every line of that list, in-house. Outsourcing is being replaced by better tools, and that is good news. Here is why.
The old calculus
The rules were almost mechanical. Every experienced manager knew them by heart.
First, size. A project had to be big enough to be worth sending outside. Small projects die in externalization: contracts, onboarding, knowledge transfer, coordination. The overhead swallows the work.
Second, competences. A company outsourced what it couldn’t do, instead of growing a skill for a one-off need.
Third, cost. Not just the day rate, but counted with the externalization overhead: a project manager on each side, quality gates, reporting layers, teams reconfigured every time the scope moved.
Fourth, focus. Companies outsourced what was not their core business, the work that added no value to their own line: support, testing, maintenance, payroll.
And fifth, flexibility. Hiring is a long commitment, and every country wraps it in its own rules and timelines. Externalization let a company scale a team up for a peak and back down after delivery, without carrying the structure in between.
The shift was visible before AI. In Deloitte’s latest global outsourcing survey, talent had become the top reason to outsource (42 percent), and cost had fallen to third (34 percent, down from 70 in 2020) [1]. Companies were buying skills. Skills are exactly what AI now sells by the token.
The new toolbox
Go back through the list. Every reason to externalize now has a better tool in-house.
Size is irrelevant. The small task that was never worth a contract is what an AI does before the coffee gets cold. No setup, no onboarding, no knowledge transfer. And the big project decomposes into small tasks the moment an AI can hold the whole picture.
Competences are on tap. The AI that writes our code and drafts our test campaigns has read more than any vendor’s bench of specialists. It works inside our walls and knows our needs, our culture, and our clients. We still need experts, just far fewer, and of a new kind: experts who steer the AI, check it, and push it further.
Cost has collapsed, and the externalization overhead went with it. No second project manager, no steering committee, no team to reconfigure. I wrote recently about how we rebuilt our project management around the AI [5]: reporting, tracking, and coordination are simply done by the machine. Tomorrow that coordination will run between AIs, and the seam between two companies becomes a protocol.
Focus is covered too. The non-core work companies used to push out, the AI now absorbs in-house for almost nothing, and it follows the company’s norms, testing, and process on every task. And flexibility is built in: capacity scales up for a peak and back down the next day, around the clock, with nothing to dismantle and nobody to let go.
Look at level 1 support. ElevenLabs sells voice agents that answer calls for about ten cents a minute, paid only while they talk [2]. No call center, not even the cheapest, can compete with that price. And the agent picks up instantly, around the clock, in any language.
Klarna showed the scale: its AI assistant took over two thirds of customer service chats in its first month, the work of 700 agents, work that used to be outsourced [3]. A year later Klarna hired humans back for the moments where customers want a person [4]. But look at what returned: flexible people working alongside the AI. The old vendor contract stayed dead.
Both sides of the contract
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Meanwhile a tell has crept into daily work. A company assigns a request to an external team, and what comes back carries the fingerprint of the same AI it runs itself. Outsourcing a task increasingly means paying someone to forward it to a model. If the work ends up in an AI anyway, why keep a man in the middle? It should be the company’s own AI, inside its walls, loaded with its context.
For the providers, the question is existential. When every client can rent world-class competence by the token, what is left to sell? A team? Clients need fewer bodies. Hours? Days billed against something that bills in seconds. The smart providers are already reinventing themselves: selling the setup, the integration, and the expertise that make these tools work, rather than the hours.
For the buyers, everything now pushes the work back home. Costs drop, speed multiplies, and the repetitive tasks nobody wanted are the first things the AI absorbs. Team motivation rises when what remains is the interesting part.
One honest caveat: internalization is not independence. We stopped giving an outsourcer a window into our company and opened one for Anthropic and OpenAI instead. The model vendors now see more of our daily work than any contractor ever did. It is a real dependency, but a dependency on infrastructure, like the cloud before it.
The company becomes local again
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here is the wider change I believe we are at the start of, and it goes beyond software.
For thirty years the logic of knowledge work was centrifugal: split the work, send the pieces where they were cheapest, manage the seams. AI reverses the direction. The advantage now lies in keeping work close to the core, where the context lives. By local I don’t mean geography. I mean local in resources: work done inside the company, by its own people and its own AI, under its own norms.
Physical externalization will survive. Nobody prompts a circuit board into existence; the fab, the factory, and the certified lab are not going anywhere. What is ending is the outsourcing of thinking: the code, the documents, the designs, the testing, the reporting.
How fast? That is the only real debate I still hear. Now, in six months, in a year? For software development it is a description of last quarter. For the rest, the capability is arriving faster than the contracts expire, and the contracts are the main thing slowing the calendar down.
So, outsourcing, replaced? For work made of information, yes. The old model is already gone; the contracts just haven’t expired yet. The make-or-buy question will outlive us all, but the answer has flipped: make it, at home, with a crew that is part human and part AI.
Companies are about to become smaller, faster, more capable, and much more local in what they are made of. I think that is one of the quiet consequences of AI, and it is the most exciting time I have known to be building things.
References

• “Global Outsourcing Survey 2024,” Deloitte (2024). Talent access cited by 42% of executives as the top driver; cost reduction third at 34%, down from 70% in 2020.

• “ElevenAgents Pricing,” ElevenLabs (2026). Voice-agent calls from roughly $0.08 to $0.10 per minute, billed on talk time.

• “Klarna AI assistant handles two-thirds of customer service chats in its first month,” Klarna press release (27 February 2024).

• “Klarna Turns From AI to Real Person Customer Service,” Bloomberg (8 May 2025).

• Guillaume Riviere, “Project Management, Rebooted,” Medium (July 2026).
